response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, I'm not on social media that much, maybe a few times a month when I remember to check, and honestly that's probably saved me a lot of headaches. The fake content thing worries me though, because when I do scroll through, I can't always tell what's real and what's complete garbage. I left school pretty early to start working with wood, so I didn't get all that media literacy training or whatever they teach now. When something pops up that seems outrageous or too perfect, it makes me anxious because I think, what if I'm believing something totally false? What if I share it and look like an idiot? I've learned to just not engage much. If something seems off or makes me feel uneasy, I just keep scrolling. I'm pretty direct with people in real life, I'll call out nonsense when I see it in the workshop or at the market, but online it's different. You can't look someone in the eye and gauge if they're being straight with you. My approach is basically to trust almost nothing I see online unless it comes from someone I actually know personally or from official sources I recognize. Even then, I double-check if it matters. My wife is better at this stuff than me, so sometimes I'll ask her opinion if something seems important. The whole fake news situation makes me want to spend even less time on these platforms, which suits me fine since I'd rather be in my garden or out on my bike anyway. I think the key is not getting sucked into the drama and remembering that most of what you see online is either trying to sell you something, make you angry, or both. Keep your real relationships strong, talk to actual people face to face, and don't let algorithms mess with your head. That's my take on it. Social media isn't the real world, and treating it like background noise rather than truth has kept me saner than a lot of people I know.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona spends minimal time on social media (few times monthly), has primary school education without advanced media literacy training, works as a carpenter (practical, hands-on profession), is anxious by nature which would amplify concerns about misinformation, has high openness (willing to consider the problem from different angles), low conscientiousness (less systematic approach, more casual), high extraversion (direct communication style, prefers real-world interaction), low agreeableness (skeptical, willing to call out nonsense, not trusting), and moderate neuroticism (some worry but not overwhelming). The opinion reflects practical wisdom from limited exposure, anxiety about being deceived, preference for real-world verification, reliance on trusted personal connections, and skepticism toward online content. The tone is conversational, direct, somewhat blunt, and reflects working-class pragmatism. References to carpentry work, gardening, cycling, and Bologna context ground the perspective in their lived experience.
response_parsed.tone: Direct, conversational, pragmatic, slightly skeptical and blunt, with underlying anxiety about deception but practical coping strategies
